planting.note: Plant corms 2–3 inches deep in fall in well-drained, humus-rich, slightly acidic to neutral woodland soil in partial shade to shade; dogtooth violet goes completely summer-dormant and requires dry conditions during its summer rest. Naturalizes readily under deciduous trees in Europe and in North American woodland gardens.

directions: Plant corms 2–3 inches deep in fall in well-drained, humus-rich, slightly acidic to neutral woodland soil in partial to full shade; dogtooth violet is the European counterpart of the North American fawn lilies — a widespread Eurasian woodland spring ephemeral producing beautifully mottled brown-and-silver basal leaves and nodding, reflexed-petaled flowers in pink, white, lilac, or rose. The common name 'dogtooth violet' comes not from the flower but from the elongated, tooth-shaped corm. The mottled foliage is as attractive as the flowers. Plant in large drifts of 20 or more under deciduous trees for best effect — single corms are easily lost, but a naturalized colony creates a stunning spring display. Goes completely dormant by late spring; suitable for underplanting with summer-dormant perennials or in spots that go dry in summer. More widely available in North American commerce than the native Erythronium species, with many named cultivars in pink, rose, purple, and white.

planting.note: Plant corms 2–3 inches deep in fall in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic, well-drained woodland soil in partial shade; pink fawn lily grows naturally in moist, shaded stream banks, redwood forest floors, and moist mixed evergreen forest of the California and Oregon Coast Ranges. Requires moisture during the growing season but tolerates summer drought when dormant.

directions: Plant corms 2–3 inches deep in fall in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic, well-drained woodland soil in partial shade; pink fawn lily is arguably the showiest of the native Pacific Coast fawn lilies, producing intensely mottled brown-and-green leaves and large, strongly reflexed, deep rose-pink flowers with a yellow center on stems 8–16 inches tall. Native to moist coastal forest and stream-bank habitats from northern California to British Columbia, it naturalizes readily in woodland gardens and increases readily from offsets to form clumps and colonies. The pink color distinguishes it from the cream-white Oregon fawn lily (E. oregonum). The cultivar 'Johnsonii' and the pink-flowered hybrid 'Pagoda' (E. revolutum x E. tuolumnense) are widely available in commerce and are excellent, robust garden plants that increase readily. Goes fully summer-dormant; plant where it won't be disturbed in summer.

planting.note: Plant corms 2–3 inches deep in fall in well-drained, slightly acidic, gravelly or loamy, humus-rich alpine or subalpine soil in partial shade; avalanche lily is a specialist of high-elevation subalpine meadows and avalanche tracks in the Cascades and Olympics, blooming as snowfields recede in early summer. Very difficult to transplant from the wild; obtain nursery-grown plants only.

directions: Plant corms 2–3 inches deep in fall in well-drained, slightly acidic, gravelly or loamy, humus-rich alpine or subalpine soil in partial shade; avalanche lily is one of the iconic wildflowers of the Pacific Northwest high country — pure white, reflexed-petaled flowers with yellow anthers that emerge through melting snowfields in early summer immediately after the snow retreats in Cascade and Olympic subalpine meadows. The sight of thousands of avalanche lilies blooming across a retreating snowfield at Mt. Rainier or the Olympics is one of the most spectacular wildflower displays in North America. Notoriously difficult to grow in lowland gardens, as it requires the very specific combination of cold winters, cool summers, and well-drained subalpine soils it evolved in. Best suited to rock gardens in the mountains; in lowland gardens, the cool, moist conditions of the Pacific Northwest coast give the best chance of success. Obtain only nursery-propagated corms; the species is protected in national parks.

planting.note: Plant corms 2–3 inches deep in fall in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, humus-rich, gravelly or rocky subalpine soil in partial shade; glacier lily grows in subalpine and alpine meadows from the Rockies through the Cascades, blooming immediately as snow melts. Challenging in lowland gardens; best in cool, high-elevation or north-facing slope sites.

directions: Plant corms 2–3 inches deep in fall in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, humus-rich, gravelly or rocky subalpine soil in partial shade; glacier lily is the yellow-flowered counterpart of the white avalanche lily, both species blooming immediately as snow retreats in Rocky Mountain and Cascade subalpine meadows. The brilliant golden-yellow, reflexed petals distinguish it from the white E. montanum. The unfussy, elegant flowers with their swept-back petals in clear yellow are among the most beautiful of the high mountain wildflowers. Grizzly bears dig the corms in large quantities in the Rockies and Yellowstone, making the species an important grizzly food source. Columbian ground squirrels also harvest the corms and seeds extensively. The plant's ecological associations in subalpine meadow ecosystems make it difficult to grow successfully in lowland gardens, but in appropriately cool, rocky, well-drained mountain garden settings it naturalizes to form striking colonies.

planting.note: Plant divisions or nursery-grown plants in spring in well-drained, slightly acidic, sandy or gravelly, lean soil in full sun to partial shade; bear grass grows naturally in dry open coniferous forests and subalpine meadows of the Pacific Northwest and northern Rockies. Individual plants bloom only every 5–7 years; divide for faster establishment.

directions: Plant divisions in spring in well-drained, slightly acidic, sandy or gravelly, lean soil in full sun to partial shade; bear grass is an iconic native of the Pacific Northwest coniferous forests and mountain meadows, producing dense, grass-like tufts of long, wiry, evergreen leaves that are surprisingly tough and stiff. The spectacular bloom — a tall spike of thousands of tiny white flowers rising 3–5 feet above the foliage — occurs only once every 5–7 years per individual plant, but in productive stands different plants bloom in different years, creating continuous display. The plume of creamy white flowers is one of the most distinctive sights of the Pacific Northwest high country. The leaves are traditionally woven by Coast Salish and other Pacific Northwest peoples into baskets and decorated ceremonial objects, and the leaves cut from wild stands are widely used in the floral industry. Black bears and elk browse the foliage and flowers. A striking architectural plant for dry, open woodland gardens in the Pacific Northwest.
